section.title: Release process for libCellML
section.paragraph: The purpose of this document is to describe the process for preparing, reviewing, creating, and finalising releases of libCellML using GitHub Actions Continuous Integration (CI).
The target audience of this document are the developers of libCellML who have write authority to the cellml/libcellml repository.
Releases are made using GitHub Actions CI and consist of four steps:
Each step must be performed in order. Later steps assume that all previous steps have completed successfully.

section.title: Prerequisites
section.paragraph: For all steps in the release process, you must:

list_item.paragraph: Be a maintainer (have write access) of the cellml/libcellml GitHub repository.
Be familiar with semantic versioning.
Ensure that no other release is currently in progress.
Choose the main branch as the branch to run the workflow from.

image.alt: GitHub Actions run workflow branch chooser.

figure.caption: Run workflow from branch chooser interface on GitHub.

note.paragraph: Merging pull requests while a release is under way is not recommended and has not been tested. Before starting a new release, check the repository for the presence of a branch named release-staging-v<version number>. The presence of such a branch indicates that a release is already in progress.

section.title: Branch model overview
section.paragraph: The main branch is the primary development branch where normal feature development occurs. The release branch represents the base for all released versions of libCellML. Release staging branches, named release-staging-v<version number>, are temporary branches used to assemble, review, and validate a candidate release before it is finalised.

section.title: Step 1 - Preparing the release
section.paragraph: The Prepare Release GitHub Actions workflow creates a staged release candidate based on a specified source branch and version number.
This workflow assembles all changes intended for the release into a dedicated staging branch and prepares them for review before validation and publication.

image.alt: GitHub Actions prepare release workflow interface.

section.paragraph: The workflow is manually triggered from the Actions tab of the cellml/libcellml repository.

list_item.paragraph: Navigate to the Prepare Release workflow in the GitHub Actions tab of the cellml/libcellml repository.
Enter the version number for the release.
(Optional) Specify a source branch if different from the default main branch.
Manually trigger the workflow.
Monitor the workflow run for completion and check for any failures.
Review the pull request created from the staging branch into the release branch.

section.title: Tasks performed
section.paragraph: The workflow performs the following tasks:

list_item.paragraph: Collects all changes for the release from the specified source branch.
Generates a changelog summarising the pull requests included in the release.
Updates version numbers throughout the codebase.
Organises these changes into multiple commits to simplify review.
Creates a release staging branch named release-staging-v<version number>.
Opens a pull request from the staging branch to the release branch.

section.title: Versioning
section.paragraph: Version numbers must follow semantic versioning rules. See Semantic Versioning for further information.
Examples of valid version numbers include:

list_item.paragraph: 1.0.0-alpha
1.0.0-beta

section.paragraph: The version number consists of:

list_item.paragraph: A core version (major, minor, patch).
An optional suffix indicating a development or pre-release.

section.paragraph: An official release is created without a suffix. Developer releases include a suffix and differ in that artifacts are not published to public registries or GitHub releases.
There are no strict validation checks beyond parsing; however, invalid semantic version strings will cause the workflow to fail.

section.title: Results
section.paragraph: On successful completion:

list_item.paragraph: A staging branch (release-staging-v<version number>) is created.
A pull request targeting the release branch is opened for review.

section.title: Failure handling
section.paragraph: If the workflow fails:

list_item.paragraph: The pull request will not be created.
The staging branch may still exist depending on when the failure occurred.

section.paragraph: Recommended actions:

list_item.paragraph: Review the workflow logs to identify the failure.
Delete or reuse the staging branch as appropriate.
Apply fixes to the source branch.
Re-run the workflow once the issue has been resolved.

section.title: Next step
section.paragraph: Once the staging pull request has been created and is ready for validation, proceed to Step 2 - Checking the release.

section.title: Step 2 - Checking the release
section.paragraph: The Check Release GitHub Actions workflow validates the staged release candidate created in Step 1 - Preparing the release. It ensures that the code builds correctly and that automated tests have been executed before proceeding with the release.

list_item.paragraph: Navigate to the Check Release workflow in the GitHub Actions tab of the cellml/libcellml repository.
Optionally adjust the configuration parameters for the end-to-end tests using the GitHub Actions interface.
Manually trigger the workflow.
Review the results published as comments on the staging pull request.

image.alt: GitHub Action check release manual trigger interface.

figure.caption: Check Release workflow interface from GitHub Action.

section.title: Tasks performed
section.paragraph: The workflow performs the following tasks on the staged release code:

list_item.paragraph: Builds the codebase.
Runs all unit tests.
Reports on status of unit tests.
Runs end-to-end tests.
Reports on status of end-to-end tests along with the metadata used for running the tests.

section.title: Results
section.paragraph: Results from the workflow are published as comments on the staging pull request:

list_item.paragraph: Unit test results: A summary indicating whether all tests passed.
End-to-end test results: A summary of the outcome along with the metadata used during test execution.

image.alt: GitHub Action check release unit test passing comment.

figure.caption: Example of the comment left by the Check Release Builder showing the unit tests have passed.

image.alt: GitHub Action check release end-to-end passing comment.

figure.caption: Example of the comment left by the Check Release Builder showing the unit tests have passed.

section.paragraph: These comments must be reviewed before proceeding to the next step.

section.title: Success criteria
section.paragraph: A release candidate is considered valid when:

list_item.paragraph: ✅ All unit tests pass (required).
⚠️ End-to-end tests may fail, but this has consequences.

section.paragraph: If end-to-end tests fail:

list_item.paragraph: The release may still proceed, however:
The user documentation will not be updated as part of the release process.
The metadata recorded in the workflow comment will be used by subsequent workflows to finalise documentation updates on the libcellml.org website.
If unit tests fail:
The release must not proceed.
The staged release should be removed and fixes applied to the source branch before starting the release process again.
If end-to-end tests fail, either:
Continue with the release, understanding that the user documentation will not be updated.
Or, fix the issues stemming from the end-to-end testing and re-run the workflow.

section.title: Next step
section.paragraph: Once all required checks have passed and the results have been reviewed, the release process can continue with Step 3 - Make the release.

section.title: Step 3 - Make the release
section.paragraph: The Make Release GitHub Actions workflow creates a release on GitHub and builds all the release assets.

image.alt: GitHub Action make release manual trigger interface.

figure.caption: Make Release workflow interface from GitHub Action.

section.paragraph: This workflow behaves differently depending on the type of release:

list_item.paragraph: For official releases, it publishes binaries to GitHub and external registries.
For developer (pre-)releases, it produces build artifacts only.

section.paragraph: In addition, the workflow creates a pull request to the Julia packaging repository, Yggdrasil, to updating the libCellML package. The pull request to Yggdrasil must be manually managed. Historically, the Julia package maintainers have been very good at approving and merging pull requests in a timely manner.

The Make Release workflow makes use of a classic personal access token with write access and pull request access to the cellml/Yggdrasil repository so that it can create a pull request to update the Julia package. This classic personal access token is stored as a secret (YGGDRASIL_PR_PAT) in the cellml/libcellml repository and is used by the workflow to create the pull request to Yggdrasil. It needs to be updated with a new token if the old token is revoked or expires. The current expiry date for the current token is 2026-06-23. Any maintainer of the cellml/libcellml repository can create a new token and update the secret if necessary.

list_item.paragraph: Navigate to the Make Release workflow in the GitHub Actions tab of the cellml/libcellml repository.
Manually trigger the workflow (this workflow may take up to 20 minutes to run).
Check the workflow run for any failures.
Monitor the pull request created on the Yggdrasil repository.

section.title: Tasks performed
section.paragraph: The workflow performs the following tasks

list_item.paragraph: Moves the staged code to the release branch.
Creates a release on GitHub using information from the staging branch.
Builds release assets including:
Binaries
Python wheels
Javascript package
Julia packaging update (pull request), and
source code
Publishes release assets to GitHub and public registries (for official releases only).

section.title: Failure handling
section.paragraph: If there is a failure in the workflow that release will be incomplete, but should not be considered a failed release. The only solution is to fix the problems through the normal channels and start the release process again.

section.title: Next step
section.paragraph: Once the release has been made and the assets have been published, the release process can continue with Step 4 - Finalising the release.

section.title: Step 4 - Finalising the release
section.paragraph: The Finalise Release GitHub Actions workflow completes the release process by updating the libcellml.org website, integrating release changes back into the main development branch, and cleaning up release staging artefacts.

image.alt: GitHub Action finalise release manual trigger interface.

figure.caption: Finalise Release workflow interface from GitHub Action.

section.paragraph: This workflow is the final step in the release process and performs post-release housekeeping tasks. The API and developer documentation will be updated and any user documentation that passed the end-to-end testing will be updated on the website.
If the release is considered a successful release:

list_item.paragraph: The libcellml.org website will be updated with the latest applicable documentation.
The release staging branch will be merged back into the main development branch.

section.paragraph: Regardless of the release outcome:

list_item.paragraph: The release staging branch will be deleted.
The associated pull request will be closed.
Navigate to the Finalise Release workflow in the GitHub Actions tab of the cellml/libcellml repository.
Manually trigger the workflow.
If the release was successful, enable the option to update the website and merge changes back into the main development branch.
Monitor the workflow run for completion and check for any failures.
Review the pull request created against the production branch of the libcellml.org website repository, and merge it if the updates are correct.

section.title: Tasks performed
section.paragraph: The workflow performs the following tasks:

list_item.paragraph: Generates API and developer documentation for the release and creates a pull request to update the website.
Generates user documentation for components that passed end-to-end testing and updates the existing website update pull request.
Updates the project version by appending a .post suffix.
Merges the release staging branch back into the main development branch.
Deletes the release staging branch and closes the associated draft pull request.

section.title: Failure handling
section.paragraph: If a failure occurs during this workflow, manual intervention may be required to resolve any issues. Recommended actions:

list_item.paragraph: Review the workflow logs to identify the source of the failure.
Fix any issues in the staging or documentation repositories.
Re-run the workflow once the problem has been resolved.
